parts.heading: An Unexpected Global Link
parts.body:
Greenwich, a name synonymous with time itself, holds a surprising connection to the tenure of lands across vast oceans. Royal charters for English colonists in North America, as well as those in Company Bombay and St Helena, frequently used the name of the manor of East Greenwich to describe land tenure as "free socage."

This meant that grantees of lands, even as far afield as New England, would hold them "as of his Majesty's manor of East Greenwich." It was a legal fiction, acknowledging the ruling monarch as the paramount lord of all soil, with lands outside England's physical boundaries constructively belonging to an existing royal manor. This Tudor-era tradition shaped the early legal framework of distant colonies, linking them directly to this unassuming Kentish manor. Indeed, places like East Greenwich in New Jersey, New York, and Rhode Island, and even Greenwich, Connecticut, owe their names to this historical quirk.

parts.heading: From 'Green Wic' to Royal Birthplace
parts.body:
The name 'Greenwich' itself has ancient roots, first appearing as 'Gronewic' in an Anglo-Saxon charter of 918. It is recorded as 'Grenewic' in 964 and 'Grenawic' in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle for 1013, eventually becoming 'Grenviz' in the Domesday Book of 1086. The name means 'green wic,' indicating a 'wich town' or emporium, from the Latin 'vicus.'

Before it became a royal powerhouse, Greenwich was a simple fishing town with a safe anchorage on the Thames, even as late as the reign of Henry V. Evidence of earlier settlement exists in Greenwich Park, where Bronze Age barrows, reused by Saxons in the 6th century, and a Roman villa or temple site with red paving tesserae, attest to ancient habitation. Over 300 coins, dating from emperors Claudius and Honorius to the 5th century, were found during the 1902 excavation of the Roman site. The Roman road from London to Dover, Watling Street, also crossed the high ground to the south, following an earlier Celtic route from Canterbury to St Albans.

parts.heading: Viking Siege and a Saint's Martyrdom
parts.body:
Greenwich's early history was not without significant strife. During the reign of Æthelred the Unready, the Danish fleet anchored off Greenwich in the River Thames for over three years, with their army encamped on the hill above. From this strategic position, they launched attacks on Kent, and in 1012, they captured the city of Canterbury.

Archbishop Alphege was taken prisoner and held for seven months in the Danish camp at Greenwich. When he refused to allow his 3,000 pieces of silver ransom to be paid, he was stoned to death. A miracle, the reported blossoming of a stick immersed in his blood, led to the release of his body and his subsequent sainthood. The parish church was dedicated to him in the 12th century; the present St Alfege's Church on the site, designed by Nicholas Hawksmoor, was completed in 1718.

parts.heading: Palace of Placentia's Rise
parts.body:
By 1300, a royal palace or hunting lodge already existed in Greenwich, with Edward I known to have made offerings at the chapel of the Virgin Mary. Subsequent Plantagenet monarchs were regular visitors, and Henry IV even made his will here. The manor was granted for life to Thomas Beaufort, Duke of Exeter, by Henry V.

However, it was Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester, Henry V's younger brother and regent to Henry VI, who truly transformed the estate. In 1447, he enclosed the park and erected a tower, Greenwich Castle, on the hill now home to the Royal Observatory. After Humphrey's death, Henry VI's consort, Margaret of Anjou, renamed the Thames-side palace the Palace of Placentia, or Pleasaunce. Edward IV further enlarged the palace and granted it to his queen, Elizabeth, in 1466. He also received permission from the Pope to establish a Franciscan friary, the first Observant House in England, adjacent to the palace in 1485. This friary would face suppression and re-establishment before its final demolition in 1662.

parts.heading: Tudor Royal Nursery
parts.body:
The Palace of Placentia became the principal residence of Henry VII, a period marking Greenwich as a crucial site for the Tudor dynasty. His sons, Henry (later Henry VIII) and Edmund Tudor, were born and baptised here at St Alphege's. Henry VIII preferred Greenwich over Eltham Palace, its location on the River Thames making it more accessible. He expanded the palace, making it his main London seat until Whitehall Palace was built in the 1530s.

Greenwich witnessed significant royal events: Henry VIII married both Catherine of Aragon and Anne of Cleves here. It was also the birthplace of his two daughters, Mary, born on 18 February 1516, and Elizabeth, born on 7 September 1533. Tragically, his son Edward VI also died at Greenwich at the age of 15. The palace remained a favoured summer residence for Elizabeth I, who, along with her sister Mary, used it extensively; Elizabeth's Council even planned the Spanish Armada campaign here in 1588. Its status as a royal possession, with a useful hill, ultimately led to its selection as the site for Charles II's Royal Observatory, establishing Greenwich's future global role.

parts.heading: Wren's Masterpiece and Naval Heritage
parts.body:
The Palace of Placentia saw its final remodelling under James I, who granted the manor to his wife, Queen Anne of Denmark. In 1616, Anne commissioned Inigo Jones to design and build the Queen's House, a surviving testament to this era. Charles I continued this patronage, granting the manor to his wife, Queen Henrietta Maria, for whom Jones completed the Queen's House.

However, the English Civil War brought an abrupt end to the palace's glory, with it being used as a biscuit factory and prisoner-of-war camp. During the Interregnum, the palace and park were seized for the Lord Protector. By the Restoration, the Palace of Placentia was in such disuse that it was pulled down. Charles II began work on a grand new palace, though only the King Charles block was completed. He also redesigned Greenwich Park and established the Royal Observatory. Prince James, later King James II, inspired the idea for a Royal Naval Hospital, which his daughter Mary II later commissioned Christopher Wren to design in 1692–1693. This Royal Hospital for Seamen, now the Old Royal Naval College, was completed by Hawksmoor under William III and continued to be patronised by Queen Anne and Prince George of Denmark.

parts.heading: A Public Resort and Global Time
parts.body:
By the 18th century, Greenwich had become a popular resort, leading to the construction of grand houses like Vanbrugh Castle (1717) and the Ranger's House (1722). Georgian estates of houses were built above the town centre, reflecting its growing appeal. The Royal Hospital for Seamen benefited from the forfeited estates of the Jacobite Earl of Derwentwater, granted by George II, allowing its completion by 1751.

In 1805, George III granted the Queen's House to the Royal Naval Asylum, an orphanage school which later amalgamated with the Greenwich Hospital School. Queen Victoria renamed it the Royal Hospital School in 1892. The Royal Observatory, founded by Charles II, established the Greenwich Meridian in 1851, solidifying Greenwich's global role as the originator of modern Prime Meridian and Greenwich Mean Time.

parts.heading: Victorian Growth and Modern Legacy
parts.body:
The 19th century saw Greenwich become part of Victorian London's growing conurbation. In 1838, the London and Greenwich Railway completed London's first steam railway, with its terminus at London Street (now Greenwich High Road), notable as the first built specifically for passengers and the first elevated railway. South of the railway's viaduct, a Victorian pumping station, part of Joseph Bazalgette's London sewerage system, was constructed in 1864.

In 1889, the parish of Greenwich merged with others to form the Metropolitan Borough of Greenwich. The maritime connections were further celebrated in the 20th century with the siting of historic vessels, the Cutty Sark and Gipsy Moth IV, by the riverfront. The National Maritime Museum, strongly supported by George V and Queen Mary, opened in 1937 in the buildings vacated by the Royal Hospital School, cementing Greenwich's heritage. The London Borough of Greenwich became the Royal Borough of Greenwich on 3 February 2012, marking Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee and recognising its enduring links with the monarchy, the Prime Meridian, and its UNESCO World Heritage status.
